subject: Clinton Wunderly
body:

Darren K.  Indyke

From: LVJET@aol.com
Subject: Fwd: Letter
Date: Fri, 28 Sep 2007 18:15:12 GMT
To: DKIESQ@aol.com
Cc: rkahn@nysgmail.com, gmax1@ellmax.com

Jeffrey, Ghislaine, Darren, and Rich,
 
I sent Clint a message on Wednesday explaining my need for him to return to  
St. Thomas to repair autopilot on Boeing. His exact response was: “Out of 
town.,  unavailable until tomorrow, will discuss options then”.
I spoke to him  Thursday night at 8pm, he told me he had a job interview on 
Oct 6 and 7th , and  he would be unable to go on a Paris trip.  I informed him 
that it was  unacceptable at this time and I asked him to make a choice.  
Either stay  and give his full attention to JEGE or resign.
I informed Clint that his  performance as flight engineer has been documented 
by myself and I will not  jeopardize our future safety with a negative 
attitude. 
1. He has been  observed sleeping at the flight engineer panel by Mr. Epstein 
at least two  occasions
2. We had been monitoring a pressurization problem. During the  flight he did 
not monitor the cabin altitude altimeter located on the flight  engineer 
panel and allowed the cabin altitude  to climb over 10,000 feet,  sounding the 
cabin altitude warning horn before making the adjustment stop the  cabin from 
climbing. Very serious, 
3. 95% of the time, pilots have to remind  Clint to swap bleed source at 
30,000 feet on engines 1 and 2, to prevent a  compressor stall of the center 
engine.
4. Clint installed a pack cooling fan  on the ground in Newark,  45 days ago, 
he informed me that he ran the  system on the ground for 20 mins and he 
approved it for return to service, we  departed on the next flight and the cooling 
fan failed taxing out, we continued  the flight to St. Thomas, after trouble 
shooting on the ground in St. Thomas, we  learned that Clint installed the 
cooling fan wires in reverse and fan ran  backwards,  He admitted to running the 
fan for 2 seconds since he assumed  it was fixed, and it was going to rain,  if 
he ran for 1 min the pack would  overheat and noticed this immediately. He 
admitted lying to myself,  Larry  Morrison and the rest of the crew after we 
learned of this mistake.
5. He has  slammed the rear pressure bulk head door located across from 
Master bathroom in  rear of plane in frustration when I asked to assist in loading 
helicopter  luggage on arrival rather than standing around. This is a very 
heavy door with a  complicated locking mechanism which is easily damaged.
 
I received a verbal resignation last night over the phone, and waiting for  
written confirmation. 
I could not let this go any further and need to  resolve this with his 
resignation or dismissal. 
 
Sincerely,
 

Larry Visoski
Chief Pilot, J.E.G.E.  Inc.

From:LVJET@aol.com
Subject:Fwd: Letter
Date:Fri, 28 Sep 2007 18:15:12 GMT
To:DKIESQ@aol.com
Cc:rkahn@nysgmail.com, gmax1@ellmax.com

Jeffrey, Ghislaine, Darren, and Rich,
 
I sent Clint a message on Wednesday explaining my need for him to return to St. Thomas to repair autopilot on Boeing. His exact response was: “Out of town., unavailable until tomorrow, will discuss options then”.
I spoke to him Thursday night at 8pm, he told me he had a job interview on Oct 6 and 7th , and he would be unable to go on a Paris trip.  I informed him that it was unacceptable at this time and I asked him to make a choice.  Either stay and give his full attention to JEGE or resign.
I informed Clint that his performance as flight engineer has been documented by myself and I will not jeopardize our future safety with a negative attitude.
1. He has been observed sleeping at the flight engineer panel by Mr. Epstein at least two occasions
2. We had been monitoring a pressurization problem. During the flight he did not monitor the cabin altitude altimeter located on the flight engineer panel and allowed the cabin altitude  to climb over 10,000 feet, sounding the cabin altitude warning horn before making the adjustment stop the cabin from climbing. Very serious,
3. 95% of the time, pilots have to remind Clint to swap bleed source at 30,000 feet on engines 1 and 2, to prevent a compressor stall of the center engine.
4. Clint installed a pack cooling fan on the ground in Newark,  45 days ago, he informed me that he ran the system on the ground for 20 mins and he approved it for return to service, we departed on the next flight and the cooling fan failed taxing out, we continued the flight to St. Thomas, after trouble shooting on the ground in St. Thomas, we learned that Clint installed the cooling fan wires in reverse and fan ran backwards,  He admitted to running the fan for 2 seconds since he assumed it was fixed, and it was going to rain,  if he ran for 1 min the pack would overheat and noticed this immediately. He admitted lying to myself,  Larry Morrison and the rest of the crew after we learned of this mistake.
5. He has slammed the rear pressure bulk head door located across from Master bathroom in rear of plane in frustration when I asked to assist in loading helicopter luggage on arrival rather than standing around. This is a very heavy door with a complicated locking mechanism which is easily damaged.
 
I received a verbal resignation last night over the phone, and waiting for written confirmation.
 I could not let this go any further and need to resolve this with his resignation or dismissal.
 
Sincerely,
 

Larry Visoski
Chief Pilot, J.E.G.E. Inc.
